metadata.title: Why Checking the Output Won't Save You

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The pipeline diagram made it land: exposure happens at ingestion at 2 PM, and moving the review from the output to 2:06 changes nothing about 2 PM because the hazard is the processing, not the destination. I want to redesign my own workflow around that gate. Help me: (1) map a real task of mine as an ingestion-to-output pipeline and mark exactly where the sensitive file enters; (2) place a gate before ingestion instead of after the summary; (3) rewrite the task so the pre-ingestion gate is the only checkpoint that has to hold.

props.command: The pipeline diagram made it land: exposure happens at ingestion at 2 PM, and moving the review from the output to 2:06 changes nothing about 2 PM because the hazard is the processing, not the destination. I want to redesign my own workflow around that gate. Help me: (1) map a real task of mine as an ingestion-to-output pipeline and mark exactly where the sensitive file enters; (2) place a gate before ingestion instead of after the summary; (3) rewrite the task so the pre-ingestion gate is the only checkpoint that has to hold.
props.segment: Why Checking the Output Won't Save

beats.narration_text: A folder of files. Thirty student records. Your plan: hand them to Cowork, review the summary before sharing. The output looks clean. The problem happened before the summary existed.

card.copy: Why Checking the Output Won't Save You
card.sub: the gate is in the wrong place

beats.narration_text: A reasonable instinct. Review what the tool produces. Catch mistakes before they spread. The review step is right there in your workflow.
beats.scene_description: a manila folder of documents on a desk, neatly stacked — orderly, official-looking, ready to be processed
beats.image_prompt: B02, close-up of a neat stack of manila folders and paper documents on a desk, official-looking files, editorial collage style, desaturated newsprint treatment, warm cream tones, no faces

beats.narration_text: Reviewing the output before you act should let you catch the problem. By the time an output exists, the exposure is already behind you. Why doesn't the review protect you?

document.quote: Reviewing the output before you act should let you catch the problem. By the time an output exists, the exposure is already behind you. Why doesn't the review protect you?

beats.narration_text: Here is the pipeline. A sensitive file enters a session. The session processes it. An output arrives. Then the human review.

viz.label: left-to-right: FILE IN → SESSION → OUTPUT → REVIEW; neutral colors, all boxes visible
viz.note: the baseline pipeline before any highlighting

beats.narration_text: The exposure is not at the output. It is at ingestion. The moment the file enters the session, the processing has occurred. The summary is downstream.

viz.label: same pipeline; CRIMSON flash at FILE IN → SESSION step; REVIEW box dimmed/grayed = too late

beats.narration_text: For sensitive data, the hazard is the processing, not the destination. Moving the output somewhere safe does not undo the ingestion.

viz.label: SESSION box highlighted CRIMSON with label 'HAZARD'; OUTPUT box neutral with label 'destination'

beats.narration_text: Exposure occurs at ingestion. A review placed at the end sits downstream of the event it was meant to prevent.

document.quote: Exposure occurs at ingestion. A review placed at the end sits downstream of the event it was meant to prevent.

beats.narration_text: Two gate positions. One after the output — that is where review-first puts the gate. One before ingestion — that is the only position that prevents the exposure.

viz.label: row A (CRIMSON gate symbol after OUTPUT — wrong); row B (TEAL gate symbol before FILE IN — correct)

beats.narration_text: The polished summary looks like a safe output. It is. The data it summarized was not safe to process.
beats.scene_description: a clean formatted summary printout on a desk, neat and professional-looking — apparently safe
beats.image_prompt: B10, close-up of a clean formatted document summary printout on a desk, bold headings and organized sections, looks safe and professional, editorial collage style, desaturated newsprint treatment, cream and charcoal tones, no faces

beats.narration_text: Illustrative. Thirty accommodation PDFs enter a personal session at two PM. That is the moment of exposure. The summary arrives at two-oh-four. The reviewer reads it at two-oh-six.

viz.label: horizontal timeline: 2:00 (CRIMSON — FILE IN / EXPOSURE) → 2:04 (OUTPUT) → 2:06 (REVIEW); 'illustrative' label
viz.note: THE EXAMPLE — time-stamped exposure vs review

beats.narration_text: Moving the review to two-oh-six changes nothing about two PM. The gate is downstream of what it was meant to gate. The only effective gate position is before the folder opens.

viz.label: same timeline; CRIMSON marker stays fixed at 2:00; TEAL gate symbol placed before 2:00 = correct; 'illustrative'
viz.note: THE EXAMPLE continued — gate must precede 2:00

beats.narration_text: Moving the gate doesn't change what happened at ingestion.

document.quote: Moving the gate doesn't change what happened at ingestion.

beats.narration_text: The gate belongs before the file goes in.

card.copy: The gate belongs before the file goes in.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Moving the gate doesn't change what happened at ingestion. The gate belongs before the file goes in.

props.artifactHeading: Why Checking the Output Won't Save You
artifactLines: Moving the review to two-oh-six changes nothing about two PM.
Moving the gate doesn't change what happened at ingestion.
The gate belongs before the file goes in.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
